claim: Annealing at 550 °C for 5 h enhances yield strengths of all strips due to more carbide precipitations.
evidence_text: Annealing at 550 °C for 5 h enhances yield strengths of all strips due to more carbide precipitations.

claim: The measured compositions (wt.%) of the strips are: 0Cr: Fe bal., Mn 26.6, Al 9.1, C 1.2; 3Cr: Fe bal., Mn 27.6, Al 8.4, C 1.3, Cr 2.8; 6Cr: Fe bal., Mn 29.1, Al 9.9, C 1.2, Cr 6.8; 9Cr: Fe bal., Mn 27.7, Al 9.5, C 1.3, Cr 9.3.
evidence_text: Table 1 Measured compositions of the studied steel strips in this work (wt. %). 0Cr Bal. 26.6 9.1 1.2 - 3Cr Bal. 27.6 8.4 1.3 2.8 6Cr Bal. 29.1 9.9 1.2 6.8 9Cr Bal. 27.7 9.5 1.3 9.3

claim: Near-rapidly solidified strips were produced by pouring melt into a copper mold rotating at 600 r/min under Ar, with maximum cooling rate approximately 5×10^3 K/s, yielding strip dimensions of (60–80) mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm.
evidence_text: The alloy ingot was reheated to melt by an induction coil, and then liquid metal was poured into a copper mold at a rotating speed of 600 r/min in Ar atmosphere, with calculated maximum cooling rate at about $5 \times 10^3$ K/s [31]. The obtained strip size was (60–80) mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm.

claim: Tensile specimens had gauge dimensions of 20 mm × 4 mm × 1 mm and were tested at a strain rate of 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ using a 10 kN capacity tester with at least three specimens per condition.
evidence_text: Tensile specimens were made in a gauge size of 20 mm × 4 mm × 1 mm, and tensile tests were conducted at a strain rate of $1 \times 10^{-3} s^{-1}$ by a MTS Criterion Model 44 mechanical property tester with a 10 kN capacity, each test data was supported by more than three tensile specimens.

claim: For the 0Cr as-cast strip, YS = 724 ± 16 MPa, UTS = 924 ± 24 MPa, TE = 40.8 ± 8%; after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, YS = 882 ± 6 MPa, UTS = 934 ± 4 MPa, TE = 30.1 ± 2%, corresponding to a YS increment of +158 MPa and a TE increment of -10.7%.

For the 3Cr as-cast strip, YS = 617 ± 4 MPa, UTS = 896 ± 28 MPa, TE = 33.2 ± 7%; after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, YS = 768 ± 9 MPa, UTS = 915 ± 23 MPa, TE = 37.4 ± 5%, corresponding to a YS increment of +151 MPa and a TE increment of +4.2%.

For the 6Cr as-cast strip, YS = 652 ± 5 MPa, UTS = 953 ± 9 MPa, TE = 31.6 ± 1%; after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, YS = 751 ± 2 MPa, UTS = 965 ± 2 MPa, TE = 44.3 ± 3%, corresponding to a YS increment of +101 MPa and a TE increment of +12.7%.

For the 9Cr as-cast strip, YS = 740 ± 8 MPa, UTS = 979 ± 22 MPa, TE = 20.3 ± 5%; after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, YS = 805 ± 11 MPa, UTS = 994 ± 21 MPa, TE = 22.8 ± 4%, corresponding to a YS increment of +65 MPa and a TE increment of +2.5%.

Quantitative measurements by Gatan Digital Micrograph show that the ferrite volume fraction is 10.2 vol%, 11.4 vol%, 14.8 vol%, and 19.6 vol% for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips, respectively, and that Cr addition changes the ferrite shape from rodlike to block like gradually; all ferrites are located in the center of austenite grains, indicating δ-ferrite nucleated first during near-rapid solidification.
evidence_text: According to a quantitative measurement by Gatan Digital Micrograph, the ferrite fraction is 10.2 vol %, 11.4 vol%, 14.8 vol%, and 19.6 vol% for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips, respectively. Moreover, Cr addition changes the shape of ferrite from rodlike to block like gradually. It is found from Fig. 2(a) that all ferrites are in the center of austenite grains, which means that δ-ferrite first nucleated and grew from melt during the near-rapid solidification.

claim: A clear superlattice of κ-carbides is observed in austenite of 0Cr strip, while the superlattice in 3Cr and 6Cr strips is very weak and no κ-carbide superlattice is detected in 9Cr strip; κ-carbides are too small to identify and only visible at the austenite/ferrite phase boundary in the DF image of 0Cr strip (Fig. 4e), indicating Cr suppresses κ-carbide precipitation.
evidence_text: There is a clear superlattice in austenite matrix of 0Cr strip, which is characterized as κ-carbides. But the superlattice in austenite diffraction pattern of 3Cr and 6 Cr strips is very weak... No superlattice belonging to κ-carbides is observed in austenite diffraction pattern of 9Cr strip... κ-carbides are only visible at phase boundary between austenite and ferrite in dark-field (DF) image of 0Cr strip, as shown in Fig. 4(e). Thus, addition of Cr suppresses the precipitation of κ-carbide

claim: In the 9Cr strip, Cr23C6 carbides are observed at austenite grain boundaries; they have a cubic structure with a lattice constant of 1.054 nm, granular or banded shapes with a width of about 100 nm, and may cause inter-granular cracking.
evidence_text: some Cr23C6 carbides are also observed at austenite grain boundary in 9Cr strip, which has a cubic structure with a lattice constant of 1.054 nm, as shown in Fig. 4(f). The Cr23C6 carbides are in granular or banded shapes, with a width of about 100 nm, which may lead to inter-granular cracking along coarsening carbides at grain boundary

claim: In 6Cr and 9Cr strips, a superlattice belonging to DO3 phase is observed in ferrite, and nano-scale DO3 phases are clearly visible in DF images, whereas no superlattice is observed in ferrite of 0Cr and 3Cr strips.
evidence_text: In 6Cr and 9Cr strips, a superlattice belonging to DO3 phase in ferrite is observed, as shown in Fig. 5 (c) and (d), which is determined by the fact that (hkl) are all odd or all even, and the reflections belong to DO3 phase are similar with previous results. According to DF images of ferrite, nano-scale DO3 phases clearly appear in ferrite of 6Cr and 9Cr strips.

claim: Reduction of nano-scale κ-carbides could lower yield strength, while precipitation of Cr23C6 and increase in dislocation density increase yield strength; the non-monotonic yield strength trend is attributed to the balance of these effects.
evidence_text: Reduction of nano-scale κ-carbides could lower down their yield strengths [14,17], while precipitation of Cr23C6 and increase in dislocation density increased their yield strengths.

claim: Reduced precipitation of κ-carbides in Cr-containing strips, which otherwise obstruct dislocation glide and generate slip bands, results in a high strain hardening rate; the glide plane softening effect of nano κ-carbides yields a lower strain hardening rate in the Cr-free strip.
evidence_text: Because of the obstruction of κ-carbides to the dislocation glide and generation of the slip bands, reduced precipitation of the κ-carbides in Cr-containing strips results in a high strain hardening rate... Glide plane softening effect of nano κ-carbides yields a lower straining hardening rate of as-cast Cr-free strip

claim: Increased δ-ferrite fraction blocks dislocation propagation, and increased dislocation density and formation of Cr23C6 are likely factors decreasing plasticity.
evidence_text: Increase of δ-ferrite fraction in Cr-containing strips can blocks the dislocation propagation due to more phase-interface areas, which may decrease the plasticity... The increased dislocation density in Cr-containing strips and formation of Cr23C6 carbides in 9Cr are the likely factors decreasing the plasticity.

claim: After annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, the constituent phases of all Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips remain austenite and ferrite.
evidence_text: The constituent phase of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips after annealing at 550 °C for 5 h are still austenite and ferrite, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7.

claim: In XRD, the (111) diffraction peak of κ-carbides at 41.4° appears in the annealed 0Cr strip, indicating that annealing promotes κ-carbide precipitation.
evidence_text: The XRD result in Fig. 6 displays that the diffraction peak (111) of κ-carbides at 41.4° obviously appears in annealed 0Cr strip, suggesting that annealing at 550 °C for 5 h promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides.

claim: Compared with the as-cast strips, the size and fraction of ferrite in the annealed strips almost remain the same.
evidence_text: Compared with the as-cast strips, the size and fraction of ferrite in annealed strips almost remain the same.

claim: Annealing at 550 °C for 5 h significantly enhances the yield strength of all Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips, while Cr addition reduces the increment of yield strength.
evidence_text: It can be found that annealing at 550 °C for 5 h significantly enhances yield strength of all the strips, while Cr addition reduces the increment of yield strength.

claim: After annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, clear superlattice spots of κ-carbides with obviously increased intensity are present in the austenite of the 0Cr strip.
evidence_text: After annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, there are still clear superlattices of κ-carbides in austenite matrix of 0Cr strip, and its intensity increases obviously as shown in Fig. 9 (a).

claim: In the SADP of annealed 3Cr and 6Cr strips, the superlattices of κ-carbides remain very weak.
evidence_text: The superlattices of κ-carbides in SADP of annealed 3Cr and 6Cr strips are still very weak, as shown in Fig. 9 (b, c).

claim: In the annealed 9Cr strip, no κ-carbide superlattice is observed; instead, only Cr23C6 carbides with a width of about 200 nm are present at grain boundaries.
evidence_text: No superlattice of κ-carbides in SADP is found, and only Cr23C6 carbides with a width of about 200 nm are observed in annealed 9Cr strip, as shown in Fig. 9 (f).

claim: After annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, superlattice reflections belonging to the DO3 phase appear in the ferrite of all strips, including 0Cr and 3Cr.
evidence_text: After annealing at 550 °C for 5 h, the superlattice belonging to DO3 phase appears in all annealed strips, including 0Cr and 3Cr, as shown in Fig. 10.

claim: The increase of κ-carbides reduces plasticity, and nano-scale κ-carbide precipitation leads to a lower strain hardening rate due to the glide plane softening effect.
evidence_text: The increase of κ-carbides can reduce plasticity... The precipitation of nano-scale κ-carbides leads to lower strain hardening rate due to glide plane softening effect [4,17,32].

claim: The presence of Cr23C6 carbide particles at grain boundaries is the main reason for the poor ductility and high strain hardening rate of both as-cast and annealed 9Cr strips.
evidence_text: The appearance of Cr23C6 carbide particles at grain boundary is the main reason for the poor ductility and high strain hardening rate of as-cast and annealed 9Cr strips.

claim: κ-carbides are mainly precipitated in the first hour of annealing at 550 °C, whereas recovery proceeds more slowly; to improve plasticity the annealing time should be prolonged to reduce dislocation density, but longer times may coarsen κ-carbides.
evidence_text: The evolution of properties and microstructures as increasing of annealing time at 550 °C indicates that κ-carbides are mainly precipitated in the first hour of annealing treatment, while the recovery needs longer time. So to improve the plasticity, the annealing time should be prolonged to reduce dislocation density at this annealing temperature, while longer annealing time may coarsen κ-carbides [34].

claim: Under the same solidification condition, the reduction of κ-carbide fraction and size increases the dislocation formation ability due to similar thermal stress, leading to higher dislocation density in Cr-containing strips; in the 9Cr strip, Cr23C6 precipitation reduces dislocation density.
evidence_text: Under the same solidification condition, the residual stress of each strips caused by rapid cooling is similar [30]. Moreover, reduction of fraction/size of nano-scale κ-carbides increases the formation ability of dislocations under the same thermal stress... while the precipitation of Cr23C6 in the 9Cr sample reduce the formation of dislocations [33,36], leading to reduction of dislocations in austenite.

claim: Cr23C6 carbides are generated when the Cr content reaches 9 wt% in the Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C steel.
evidence_text: it is found that Cr23C6 carbides are generated when the Cr content reaches to 9 wt %.

claim: After tensile fracture, average slip band spacing in as-cast 0Cr strip is ~90 nm and in as-cast 6Cr strip is ~80 nm.
evidence_text: Average spacing between slip bands is ~90 nm and ~80 nm in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips, respectively.

claim: Narrower slip band spacing (~80 nm in 6Cr) is associated with higher strain hardening, but does not lead to higher elongation due to the high initial dislocation density in as-cast 6Cr compared to 0Cr (~90 nm).
evidence_text: The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually suggests higher strain hardening [32,42,43], which is in a good agreement with our results. The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually causes higher elongation after tensile fracture, which is not consistent with our work, because 0Cr strip with larger average spacing has higher elongation than that of 6Cr strip.

claim: After tensile fracture, average slip band spacing in annealed 0Cr strip increases to ~190 nm, while in annealed 6Cr strip it decreases to ~60 nm, consistent with increased elongation in 6Cr and decreased elongation in 0Cr.
evidence_text: average spacing of 0Cr strip increases to ~190 nm, but that of 6Cr strip decreases to ~60 nm compared with as-cast strips, which is also evidenced by increase in elongation of 6Cr strip and decrease in elongation of 0Cr strip.

claim: The average width of slip bands is inversely correlated with spacing between slip bands after tensile fracture.
evidence_text: These indicate that average width of slip bands is inversely correlated with spacing between slip bands after tensile fracture.

claim: Cr23C6 carbides precipitated in 9Cr strip instead of κ-carbides.
evidence_text: Cr23C6 carbides precipitated in 9Cr strip, instead of κ-carbides.

claim: With increasing Cr content, yield strength of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips first decreased due to reduction of κ-carbides, and then gradually increased due to increase in dislocation density and formation of Cr23C6 carbides.
evidence_text: With an increase of Cr content, yield strength of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips decreased first due to reduction of κ-carbides, and then increased gradually because of the increase in dislocation density and the formation of Cr23C6 carbides.

claim: The increase in dislocation density and formation of Cr23C6 carbides gradually reduced the ductility of Cr-containing strips.
evidence_text: The increase in dislocation density and formation of Cr23C6 carbides reduced the ductility of Cr-containing strips gradually.

claim: Annealing at 550 °C for 5 h promoted the precipitation of κ-carbides, but the content and size of κ-carbides in Cr-containing strips remained lower than those in the Cr-free strip.
evidence_text: Annealing at 550 °C for 5 h promoted the precipitation of κ-carbides, but content and size of κ-carbides in Cr-containing strips were still lower than those of Cr-free strip.

claim: The formation of Cr23C6 carbides caused the lowest ductility of both as-cast and annealed 9Cr strips.
evidence_text: The formation of Cr23C6 carbides caused the lowest ductility of as-cast and annealed 9Cr strips.
